steps.title: Identify what the blank fills and the rule that applies
steps.content: The sentence reads: "her 2019 novel ______ offers a delicious twist..." The blank is the novel’s title, which sits between the noun phrase "her 2019 novel" and the verb "offers." Standard English does not place any punctuation between a subject and the verb that immediately follows it; the title simply slots in with no mark on either side.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — Gingerbread—: An em dash after the title interrupts the subject before its verb, creating an incomplete construction. ✗
  • Choice B — Gingerbread,: A comma between the subject and the verb is never acceptable in standard English grammar. ✗
  • Choice C — Gingerbread: The title alone, with no following punctuation, allows "her 2019 novel Gingerbread" to function smoothly as the subject of "offers." ✓
  • Choice D — Gingerbread:: A colon after the title implies the verb phrase will define or list something introduced by the title, which is grammatically inappropriate here. ✗
C is correct.
